title: Database — Query & Table Performance
description: Forensic companion to the 'Database — Server Health' dashboard: which query and which table is responsible? Expand the 'How to read this dashboard' row at the top for leaderboard-integrity caveats and data-source details.

list.name: BOOTSTRAP_REGEX
list.description: Hidden constant. Single source of truth for the bootstrap/driver-noise regex referenced by the three Top-N leaderboards (panels 1, 2, 3). Uses RE2 character classes ([$], [.], [(], [)]) instead of backslash escapes so it survives Grafana's JSON round-trip without double-escaping. When a new JDBC driver version introduces a novel introspection query that dominates the leaderboards after a quiet period, extend the pattern HERE, not in each panel separately — the three panels disagreeing on what counts as 'noise' is the whole failure mode this variable exists to prevent.
list.query: (?i)^(BEGIN|COMMIT|ROLLBACK|SET .*|SHOW .*|SELECT [$][0-9]+( AS TABLE_CAT)?[ ]*$|SELECT t[.]oid AS oid.*|SELECT DISTINCT ON [(]typname[)].*|SELECT COALESCE[(]SUM[(]GREATEST[(]child[.]reltuples.*).*

current.value: (?i)^(BEGIN|COMMIT|ROLLBACK|SET .*|SHOW .*|SELECT [$][0-9]+( AS TABLE_CAT)?[ ]*$|SELECT t[.]oid AS oid.*|SELECT DISTINCT ON [(]typname[)].*|SELECT COALESCE[(]SUM[(]GREATEST[(]child[.]reltuples.*).*

options.content:
### Purpose
Forensic companion to the **Database — Server Health** dashboard. Server Health answers *is the DB healthy right now?*; this dashboard answers *which query and which table is responsible?*.

### Data sources

| Panel group | Source | Collector config |
|---|---|---|
| Top-N query leaderboards | `pg_stat_statements` via **postgres-exporter** | `--collector.stat_statements`, `--collector.stat_statements.include_query` |
| Table-level panels (dead-tuple, index-scan, autovacuum, HOT) | `pg_stat_user_tables` via **postgres-exporter** | default `stat_user_tables` collector |
| Integrity tiles (evictions, visible %) | `pg_stat_statements_info` + row count via **sql-exporter** | `docker/sql-exporter/pg-stat-statements-info.collector.yml` |
| Block-I/O panel | `pg_stat_io` via **sql-exporter** | `docker/sql-exporter/wal-io.collector.yml` |

### Leaderboard integrity — two truncation mechanisms

The three Top-N leaderboards are subject to two independent truncations, each with an integrity tile at the top of the dashboard:

1. **Exporter-side (common).** postgres-exporter v0.19.1 hardcodes `ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC LIMIT 100` with no override flag. Once PG tracks >100 normalized statements the leaderboards silently omit the tail, including call-rate-hot but exec-time-cheap queries (the classic N+1 signature). The **% visible** tile catches this.
2. **PG-side LRU (rare).** `pg_stat_statements` is bounded by `pg_stat_statements.max` (pinned at 5000 in `docker-compose.local.yml`). When the cap is hit PG ages entries out in LRU order and the full in-DB view becomes a moving target. The **LRU eviction** tile catches this.

Both integrity metrics come from sql-exporter because postgres-exporter covers the main `pg_stat_statements` view only, not `pg_stat_statements_info`. If investigation needs the clipped tail, query pg_stat_statements directly via psql rather than calling `pg_stat_statements_reset()` (which erases all history).

### Why integrity tiles and traffic sentinel sit at the top

Reading a truncated leaderboard as if it were complete is the most common diagnostic mistake this dashboard can cause — the tiles are pinned so the eye lands on integrity signals *before* the ranked data. The **Application traffic** sentinel alongside disambiguates an empty leaderboard: green sentinel + empty leaderboards = real filtering bug; yellow sentinel + empty leaderboards = the 15m window rolled past the last workload.

### PromQL shapes and load-bearing details

- **Top-N join shape.** `sum by (queryid) (rate(...)) * on(queryid) group_left(query) pg_stat_statements_query_id`. The `sum by (queryid)` wrapper is mandatory — without it the many-to-one match fails because `datname`/`user` labels appear on the left but not the right, and the panel silently returns "No data".
- **`or vector(0)` + outer `sum()`.** On the two integrity tiles, `sum(...) or vector(0)` collapses the metric-side labels to the same empty label-set as the fallback so PromQL's `or` returns one series, not two. Do not remove the `sum()` — it's a correctness guard, not aggregation.
- **`BOOTSTRAP_REGEX` dashboard variable.** Hidden constant that filters driver noise (BEGIN/COMMIT/SHOW, JDBC introspection) from the three leaderboards. Uses RE2 character classes (`[$]`, `[.]`, `[(]`) instead of backslash escapes to survive Grafana's JSON round-trip. If a new driver version introduces novel noise, extend the pattern there — three panels disagreeing on what counts as noise is the failure mode this variable exists to prevent.
- **Traffic sentinel filter.** Uses `user!="monitoring"` (denylist) rather than `user="wealthpay"` (allowlist) because the app's DB user is literally the string `user` (see `.env` `DB_USER=user`).
- **`track_io_timing=on` required.** The Top-20 block-I/O panel is permanently 0 without it — not a bug, a missing server setting.
- **`pg_stat_io` is cluster-scoped.** No `datname` filter on the block-reads-by-backend panel: pg_stat_io has no per-database column, so adding one matches zero series.
- **Window choice.** 15m for ranking leaderboards (stable across a single Gatling run), 5m for timeseries (reactive to regressions). The two windows are deliberately different.

### Table-level panel caveats

- **Dead-tuple ratio size gate ≥1000 rows.** Below that, `flyway_schema_history` and `cron.*` dominate on ratio noise; autovacuum's own tuple-count threshold hasn't activated either, so nothing below the gate is actionable.
- **Index-scan ratio rank-then-show.** Ranks by combined scan activity first, then shows the ratio only for those 10 tables — a naive `topk(10, ratio)` picks barely-used tables at 100% and displaces the hot ones.
- **Autovacuum staleness has no thresholds deliberately.** There's no universal correct cadence. Read alongside dead-tuple ratio: both rising on the same table = autovacuum falling behind; staleness rising with dead-tuple ratio flat = table is quiet.
- **HOT ratio scoped to `account_balance_view`.** `event_store` and `outbox` are append-only via BEFORE UPDATE triggers, so `n_tup_upd` is structurally zero and HOT% is undefined there.

### Per-table expectations under this workload

A dip on any of the hot tables' **index-scan ratio** under load = planner regression worth investigating; cross-reference with the 'Top-20 queries by total exec time' leaderboard above.

| Table | Dead-tuple ratio (expected) | Index-scan ratio (expected) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `event_store` | **0%** | ≈100% on `(aggregate_id, version)` | Append-only via BEFORE UPDATE trigger. A non-zero dead ratio = trigger bypassed. |
| `account_snapshot` | Low and stable | ≈100% on `(aggregate_id)` | Snapshot upsert; occasional UPDATE expected. |
| `account_balance_view` | **Rises under Gatling** | ≈100% on `(account_id)` | Projection target — one UPDATE per event. Primary bloat watchlist; pair with HOT panel. |
| `outbox_YYYY_MM_DD` | Rises during CDC lag, then drops | ≈100% on `(id)` | Partitioned. Bloat reclaimed by pg_cron partition drops (see ADR: Outbox Lifecycle Management). |
| `processed_transactions` / `processed_reservations` | Low and stable | ≈100% on idempotency composite | Append-mostly; used for idempotency lookups per command. |

### PG18 hazard
On PG18 the `wal` object's counters relocate from `pg_stat_wal` into `pg_stat_io` rows with `object='wal'`. The block-reads panel here keeps rendering, but the Server Health dashboard's WAL latency panels need re-wiring. See `docs/postgres-upgrade.md`.

panels.title: pg_stat_statements LRU eviction (15m)
panels.description: Entries aged out of pg_stat_statements in the last 15m — non-zero means the PG-side leaderboard is a moving target. Expected to be 0 on this stack.

panels.title: % of tracked statements visible in leaderboards
panels.description: Share of tracked statements visible in the leaderboards below — <80% means postgres-exporter's LIMIT 100 is clipping the tail (typically N+1-style cheap-but-chatty queries).

panels.title: Application traffic — last 15m
panels.description: Application call rate over the last 15m — disambiguates an empty leaderboard from a broken filter. Green = traffic present; yellow = window rolled past the last workload.

panels.title: Top-20 queries by total exec time (rate, 15m)
panels.description: Seconds of backend CPU per wall-clock second, per query over 15m — 1.0 = one full core pinned by a single query.

targets.expr: topk(20, sum by (queryid) (rate(pg_stat_statements_seconds_total{datname="wealthpay", user!="monitoring"}[15m])) * on(queryid) group_left(query) pg_stat_statements_query_id unless on(queryid) pg_stat_statements_query_id{query=~"${BOOTSTRAP_REGEX}"})

panels.title: Top-20 queries by call rate (15m)
panels.description: Calls per second per query — surfaces N+1 patterns that the exec-time leaderboard misses because cheap × many is still less than one slow query.

targets.expr: topk(20, sum by (queryid) (rate(pg_stat_statements_calls_total{datname="wealthpay", user!="monitoring"}[15m])) * on(queryid) group_left(query) pg_stat_statements_query_id unless on(queryid) pg_stat_statements_query_id{query=~"${BOOTSTRAP_REGEX}"})

panels.title: Top-20 queries by block I/O time (15m)
panels.description: Seconds in shared-buffer or disk I/O per query — isolates I/O-bound from CPU-bound queries. Requires `track_io_timing=on`.

targets.expr: topk(20, sum by (queryid) (rate(pg_stat_statements_block_read_seconds_total{datname="wealthpay", user!="monitoring"}[15m]) + rate(pg_stat_statements_block_write_seconds_total{datname="wealthpay", user!="monitoring"}[15m])) * on(queryid) group_left(query) pg_stat_statements_query_id unless on(queryid) pg_stat_statements_query_id{query=~"${BOOTSTRAP_REGEX}"})

panels.title: Dead-tuple ratio — top 10 tables (≥1000 rows)
panels.description: Fraction of dead tuples awaiting autovacuum, for tables with ≥1000 rows. Primary bloat signal for the event-sourced + outbox workload.

panels.title: Index-scan ratio — top 10 tables by scan activity
panels.description: Index-scan fraction for the 10 most-scanned tables (5m) — a dip on a hot table (event_store, account_snapshot, account_balance_view, outbox) signals a planner regression. Low ratio on small tables is expected.

targets.expr: (rate(pg_stat_user_tables_idx_scan{datname="wealthpay", schemaname=~"account|public"}[5m]) / (rate(pg_stat_user_tables_idx_scan{datname="wealthpay", schemaname=~"account|public"}[5m]) + rate(pg_stat_user_tables_seq_scan{datname="wealthpay", schemaname=~"account|public"}[5m]))) and on(datname, schemaname, relname) topk(10, rate(pg_stat_user_tables_idx_scan{datname="wealthpay", schemaname=~"account|public"}[5m]) + rate(pg_stat_user_tables_seq_scan{datname="wealthpay", schemaname=~"account|public"}[5m]) > 0)

panels.title: Autovacuum staleness per table
panels.description: Age of each table's last autovacuum/autoanalyze. Read alongside dead-tuple ratio: both rising = autovacuum falling behind; staleness alone = table is quiet.

panels.title: HOT-update ratio — account_balance_view (projection target)
panels.description: HOT-update fraction on account_balance_view — expected ≈100% in steady state. A drop toward 0 means someone added an index on `balance`, `version`, or `updated_at` and broke the fast-update path.

panels.title: Block reads by backend type (pg_stat_io)
panels.description: Block reads per second by backend type (client, autovacuum, bgwriter, checkpointer, walsender) — attributes who is driving I/O right now.
